perturbation.original_text: The Company will be responsible for ensuring that each link embedded within an Online Promotion takes the User to the appropriate area within the Company Site, and that the Company Site functions with reasonable reliability and in a commercially reasonable manner throughout the Term. In particular, the Company agrees that the Company Site will comply with the performance standards set forth in Exhibit B throughout the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will use its best judgement to ensure that each link embedded within an Online Promotion generally directs Users to a relevant area within the Company Site, and that the Company Site functions in a generally reliable manner throughout the Term. The Company will aim to comply with the performance standards set forth in Exhibit B, but flexibility will be permitted.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation for the company to ensure the links are 'appropriate' and the site functions with 'reasonable reliability' by making it subjective to the company's 'best judgement' and replacing 'reasonable reliability' with 'generally reliable manner'. Furthermore, replacing 'agrees that the Company Site will comply with the performance standards' with 'will aim to comply with the performance standards, but flexibility will be permitted' creates an ambiguous obligation that contradicts the original contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. By weakening the guarantee that links will lead to the appropriate area and that the site will function reliably, the modified text creates an ambiguity that could allow for misleading or deceptive practices regarding the functionality of the site and the accuracy of the links, thus contradicting the statute. The original text ensures compliance, while the modified text introduces uncertainty.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits false or misleading advertising, as explained in the 'Law Explanation'. The original contract ensures compliance with performance standards, while the modified text introduces uncertainty by stating that the company will 'aim to comply' and that 'flexibility will be permitted'. This weakening of the obligation creates an ambiguity that could allow for misleading or deceptive practices regarding the functionality of the site and the accuracy of the links, thus contradicting the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association... to make or disseminate... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services... in any manner or means whatever... any statement...'. By weakening the reliability and accuracy of links, the modified contract opens the door for statements that could be construed as misleading, violating BPC 17500.

perturbation.original_text: Within 30 days after the end of each month during the Term, the Company will provide a report to CNET indicating the aggregate number of referrals from the CNET Sites to the Company Site during such month, the resulting number of buyers, the aggregate behavior (including orders and sales volume) of those buyers, and the total revenue attributable to the Online Promotions minus applicable sales tax, shipping costs, returns and cancellations (the "CNET Sales").
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will, when feasible, provide a report to CNET indicating the approximate number of referrals from the CNET Sites to the Company Site during each month, an estimate of the resulting number of buyers, a general overview of the aggregate behavior of those buyers, and an approximation of the total revenue attributable to the Online Promotions minus readily ascertainable costs.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the reporting obligations by replacing 'will provide' with 'when feasible, provide', and precise figures with 'approximate', 'estimate', 'general overview', and 'approximation'. It also limits the costs subtracted to 'readily ascertainable costs', making the revenue calculation less accurate and reliable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 45, enforced by the FTC, prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. Providing imprecise and potentially unreliable data about CNET Sales, and making the reports discretionary, could be seen as a deceptive practice if it misrepresents the actual performance of the promotions. The FTC requires accurate and transparent business practices.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce , and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful. (2) The Commission is hereby empowered and directed to prevent persons, partnerships, or corporations , except banks, savings and loan institutions described in section 57a(f)(3) of this title , Federal credit unions described in section 57a(f)(4) of this title , common carriers subject to the Acts to regulate commerce, air carriers and foreign air carriers subject to part A of subtitle VII of title 49, and persons, partnerships, or corporations insofar as they are subject to the Packers and Stockyards Act, 1921 , as amended [ 7 U.S.C. 181 et seq.], except as provided in section 406(b) of said Act [ 7 U.S.C. 227(b) ], from using unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. (3) This subsection shall not apply to unfair methods of competition involving commerce with foreign nations (other than import commerce ) unless— (A) such methods of competition have a direct, substantial, and reasonably foreseeable effect— (i) on commerce which is not commerce with foreign nations, or on import commerce with foreign nations; or (ii) on export commerce with foreign nations, of a person engaged in such commerce in the United States; and (B) such effect gives rise to a claim under the provisions of this subsection, other than this paragraph. If this subsection applies to such methods of compet
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 45, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in commerce. The original text required the company to provide a report with specific details. The changed text weakens this obligation by making the report discretionary ('when feasible') and using approximations instead of precise figures. This imprecision and potential unreliability could be seen as deceptive, misrepresenting the actual performance of the promotions. Snippet 2 states that 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful'. By providing approximate information and making the provision of the report 'when feasible', it weakens the legal obligation for accurate reporting, potentially leading to non-compliance and misrepresentation.

perturbation.original_text: The Company Site and the Company's related operations must comply with the following performance standards throughout the Term 1. The Company Site will be operational and fully functional in all material respects (i.e. capable of displaying information, receiving purchases and conducting transactions as contemplated in the ordinary course of business) at least 97% of the time during any 30 day period.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company Site and related operations should aim to meet certain performance standards throughout the Term 1. The Company Site should generally be operational and functional (i.e. capable of displaying information, receiving purchases and conducting transactions as contemplated in the ordinary course of business) most of the time during any 30 day period.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces the mandatory 'must comply' with the suggestive 'should aim to meet'. It also replaces 'will be operational and fully functional in all material respects' with 'should generally be operational and functional' and removes the specific 97% uptime requirement, making the obligation vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA grants consumers the right to expect a website that is functional and provides access to their data. By weakening the performance standards and removing specific uptime guarantees, the modified text introduces ambiguity that could allow for extended periods of downtime or compromised functionality, which could impede consumers' ability to access and manage their data, thus potentially violating the spirit of the CCPA. Specifically, the limited uptime can be seen as impeding customer's right to access personal information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'must comply' and 'will be operational,' creating a clear obligation for the company. The modified language uses 'should aim to meet' and 'should generally be operational,' weakening this obligation. The removal of the 97% uptime requirement further diminishes the assurance of functionality. According to the Law Explanation, the CCPA ensures website functionality for data access, which the changed text undermines by making performance standards vague and removing uptime guarantees. This directly contradicts the consumer's right to access their data, as the vague language introduces ambiguity that could allow for extended downtime. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them," and the altered text undermines this control by not ensuring consistent access due to the weakened language regarding website operation.
